title: Common Pricing of Decentralized Risk: A Linear Option Pricing Model
abstract: This paper proposes a top-down linear option pricing model that unifies the pricing of different option contracts not by assuming common dynamics but by imposing common pricing on each risk source in proportion to decentralized risk estimates. The model generates significantly better pricing performance than existing bottom-up models. Its high-dimensional risk structure effectively explains the options return variation, allowing for the seamless integration of option pricing with risk management. The market price of risk estimate from the model strongly predicts the future excess return of the corresponding risk-targeting option portfolio, an important dimension of attribute completely absent from prior literature.

author.Liuren Wu: Baruch College, The City University of New York

article.1. Common Pricing of Decentralized Risk:

SinceBlack and Scholes (1973)andMerton (1973)(henceforth BMS), the classic option pricing literature generates bottom-up valuation by specifying the risk-neutral dynamics of the underlying security price and taking expectation on the payoffs of the different option contracts based on the same underlying dynamics. The literature has proposed a long list of dynamics specifications to accommodate the different features of the underlying security price movements and the observed option price behaviors.1A key virtue of this approach is that the security price dynamics creates a common yardstick for centralizing the valuation of all derivative contracts on this security, guaranteeing that the valuations are consistent with one another relative to this dynamics.

More recently,Carr and Wu (2020)propose an alternative decentralized top-down pricing theory. Instead of starting with the security price dynamics, the theory starts with a particular option contract and generates pricing implications on this contract alone based on its own risk behaviors. The theory summarizes the value variation of the option through the BMS pricing equation in terms of variations in the underlying security price and the contract’s own BMS implied volatility. The contract’s risk exposures are constructed as partial derivatives of its BMS value representation with respect to the two observable risk sources, namely, the option’s gamma (second derivative to security price), vega (first derivative to implied volatility), volga (second derivative to implied volatility), and vanna (cross derivative to security price and implied volatility). Imposing the no-dynamic-arbitrage condition between this option contract and the underlying security generates a linear pricing relation between the option’s theta time decay (partial derivative to time) and these risk exposures, each multiplied by the risk-neutral expectation of the corresponding variation.

This top-down representation decomposes the observation of a single option contract into several features in terms of the expected excess return contribution of each risk source. The no-dynamic-arbitrage condition establishes a structural link between these features and generates a decentralized pricing relation on this particular contract, without any direct linkage to the pricing of any other contracts. The approach achieves great flexibility by decentralizing the pricing relation down to a single option contract, avoiding the tensions that a bottom-up model often encounters in trying to link all contracts together under a single dynamics specification. In fact, this decentralized pricing relation offers too much flexibility because an option’s time decay can be balanced by risk-neutral expected excess return contributions from four risk sources. Some centralization is necessary for identification.

Historically, researchers strive to achieve identification on the decentralized relation by assuming common risk-neutral implied volatility dynamics across different option contracts. If one assumes that the risk-neutral moments of the implied volatilities are the same across different option contracts, one can pool the pricing of these contracts and identify the common risk-neutral moments through a cross-sectional regression of these options’ time decay against their risk exposures. Yet, in reality, implied volatilities show different degrees of variation for options at different moneyness and maturities. As such, these pooling efforts face the classic tradeoff between the need for identification and the need for accommodating the actual behavior differences for different contracts. In the literature,Carr and Wu (2016)impose a common risk-neutral implied volatility dynamics on the whole implied volatility surface, but attempt to use some functional form to accommodate the dynamics variation across option maturity.Carr, Wu, and Zhang (2022)limit the common risk assumption to options at a single maturity and use a cross-sectional regression per each maturity to obtain partial identification.2Carr and Wu (2020)further relax the risk commonality assumption and only assume that the implied volatilities of nearby contracts share similar behaviors. They propose local-smoothing identification of the risk-neutral moments through a local linear weighted regression, with the regression weights centering around the moneyness and maturity of the target contract.

In this paper, we do not assume any common risk dynamics. Quite the opposite, we explicitly recognize the risk behavior difference of different option contracts. We use historical moment estimators on the underlying security return and the contract’s implied volatility changes as model inputs to capture their historical behavior difference. We propose to centralize the pricing of different option contracts by assuming that the market pricing per unit of the estimated risk for each risk source is the same across these contracts.

Some commonality assumption is necessary to centralize the decentralized pricing of different option contracts and achieve identification. Assuming common risk is in direct conflict with the observed risk behavior difference of different option contracts. By comparison, our common market price of risk assumption is much more generally applicable, and consistent with the general implication of the classic statistical arbitrage pricing theory (Ross 1976).

Multiplying the risk exposures of an option contract by the corresponding historical moment estimators anchors the break-even contribution of each risk source to the valuation of this contract. Relative to the break-even contribution, a risk can be priced either at a premium or at a discount. We do not impose any structural constraints on the sign or size of this premium or discount, but only require that the same premium or discount per each unit of the estimated risk be applied to all option contracts at a given point in time. By imposing common market pricing on each risk source, we unite the decentralized pricing of all option contracts into a simple linear option pricing model. At each point in time, a cross-sectional regression identifies the model. The regression slope coefficient captures the common market price of risk on each risk source. The regression residuals reflect the pricing errors of the individual option contracts in terms of their idiosyncratic pricing deviations from the common market pricing assumption.

Whereas bottom-up models generate fair option valuation relative to an estimated risk-neutral security price dynamics, our model generates fair valuation by requiring the different options contracts price the four risk dimensions consistently relative to one another at any given point in time in proportion to the decentralized risk estimates. The model does not specify the underlying security price dynamics, nor imposes any constraints on the sign, size, or time variation of the common market pricing of each risk dimension.

The top-down linear option pricing model is not only extremely simple, but also generally applicable to any types of options on any types of underlying securities, as long as historical observations are available to construct the historical moment estimators. The BMS representation and the associated risk sensitivities can be readily computed for most derivative types. The historical moment estimators represent a simple starting point to capture the risk behavior difference. Researchers can generate different statistical forecasts based on different forecasting specifications and instruments. In principle, replacing the historical moment estimators with more accurate statistical forecasts can generate better pricing performance and identify more accurate market price of risk estimates. The decentralized nature of the model allows one to incorporate any sets of statistical forecasts without the need to know the underlying information source or specification.

Our empirical analysis uses over-the-counter currency options on the US dollar against the Japanese yen and British pound, spanning the years 1996 to 2022. The data for each currency consist of 25 implied volatility quotes across a grid of five maturities at 1, 3, 6, 9, and 12 months and five strikes per each maturity at 10, 25, 50, 75, and 90 put delta. The model prices the cross-sectional variations of the currency options extremely well. The cross-sectional regression generates extremely highR2estimates, with median values at 99.9% for both currencies. The mean absolute pricing errors on the implied volatilities average merely 22 and 27 basis points on the pound and yen options, respectively, less than half of the mean absolute pricing errors from commonly designed bottom-up models.

The pricing performance represents a significant improvement over the prior literature, but the model’s strength goes far beyond its pricing performance. Its high-dimensional risk structure also explains the options excess return variation very well, allowing the model to overcome the limitations of traditional option pricing models and successfully integrate option pricing with risk management. Furthermore, the market price of risk estimates from the cross-sectional regression estimation of the model strongly predict the future realized excess returns of the corresponding risk-targeting option portfolio, an important dimension absent from prior literature.

To highlight these contributions, we construct a linear option return factor model in parallel to the linear option pricing model. The return factor model explains the cross-sectional variation of delta-hedged option excess returns with the same set of risk-adjusted exposures and the pricing error from the pricing model. A cross-sectional forecasting regression identifies the return factor model at each date. The regression slope on each risk-adjusted exposure represents the excess return of the risk-targeting option portfolio that targets a unit exposure to that risk source. The slope on the pricing error captures the excess return of a statistical arbitrage portfolio that targets a unit exposure to option misvaluation while being neutral to all risk dimensions. The model can explain a large proportion of the currency option excess return variation. The forecasting return regressionR2estimates average at 88-89% for the two currencies.

Investing in the statistical arbitrage portfolio generates highly positive excess returns with high information ratios for both currencies, highlighting the reverting nature of the pricing errors from the linear pricing model. On the other hand, the average excess returns on the different risk-targeting portfolios show different signs, positive on the vega and volga portfolios but negative on the gamma and vanna portfolios for both currencies, all with different degrees of statistical significance. The large variation in both sign and magnitude suggests that investors ask for different average compensation for different types of risk exposures.

The market price of risk estimate from the pricing model on each date represents the conditional expected excess return per unit of the corresponding risk on that date. The excess return over the next week on the corresponding risk-targeting option portfolio represents a noisy realization of this conditional expectation. When we regress the ex post realized excess return of each risk-targeting portfolio on the ex ante market price of risk estimate in a time-series forecasting regression, we find that the regression slope estimates are highly positive with strong statistical significance for all risk-targeting portfolios on both currencies, and the average intercept estimates are small and insignificant for most portfolios, broadly in line with the expectation hypothesis.

We propose a market timing strategy on each risk-targeting portfolio based on its market price of risk estimates: short the portfolio when the risk is priced at a premium and long the portfolio when it is priced at a discount. The strategy allows investors to harvest the time-varying risk premium from each risk source without relying on any predictive return regressions. It generates consistently high profitability on all risk-targeting option portfolios for both currencies.

As the classic bottom-up option pricing literature specifies increasingly sophisticated risk-neutral dynamics in an effort to match the cross-sectional variation of the option price behaviors, there is often a noticeable disconnect between the estimated risk-neutral dynamics and the actual time-series variation of the underlying security prices and option implied volatilities. The low dimensionality of the commonly designed bottom-up pricing models also severely limits their explanatory power on option return variation (Israelov and Kelly 2017). A common industry maneuver is to adopt a bottom-up pricing model for valuation, but switch to BMS sensitivities for risk management and return attribution (Bergomi 2016), effectively disintegrating the pricing effort from risk management. Our option pricing model uses the same BMS exposures as in common industry practice, and embeds statistical moment forecasts to capture how the underlying security price and each contract’s implied volatility are expected to behave. The model generates exceptional pricing performance. Its high-dimensional linear risk structure also explains options return variation very well. As such, the model overcomes the limitations of traditional bottom-up pricing models and for the first time can successfully integrate the pricing effort with actual profit and loss attribution and risk management.

Historically, researchers have also proposed many empirical return factor models across different markets, and have designed investment strategies based on rolling estimation of these models. The general strategy is to be long a factor portfolio if the rolling historical average of the excess returns on the portfolio is significantly positive, and short if the historical average excess return is significantly negative. The concern with this approach is that historical performance does not necessarily carry into the future. This is especially true when the risk premium on a risk dimension varies strongly over time and switches signs. Estimating our new option pricing model through a cross-sectional regression on any date directly generates the market price of risk estimate on that date. The estimate represents the conditional expected excess return on the corresponding risk-targeting portfolio, and can be directly applied to the market timing investment of the portfolio without any forecasting regression involved. Strong time variation in the risk premium of a risk dimension does not reduce the efficacy of the market timing strategy, but rather makes it all the more useful relative to the traditional investment strategy based on historical averages of return forecasting regressions. This is an important and unique dimension of our model absent from prior literature. Empirical return factors models can only rely on historical averages and hope for stability and continuity of risk premiums into the future. Estimating bottom-up option pricing models can sometimes generate some static market price of risk coefficients, but these coefficients are often much too stylized to be of any use for return prediction.
We consider a market with risk-free bonds, a risky financial security, and a set of option contracts written on this security. For notational simplicity, we represent the values of the security and its option contracts in their corresponding forward space. The returns on these forward securities are excess returns over the risk-free bond. We assume no dynamic arbitrage between each option contract and the underlying security, so that there exists a risk-neutral probability measureQ⁠, equivalent to the statistical probability measureP⁠, such that the expected excess returns on the security and the option contract are zero.

1.1 Top-down representation and P&L attribution

Instead of specifying the security price dynamics and generating option valuation from bottom up, we start top down with an option contractiat a given datet⁠, and represent its value variation through the BMS pricing equation,Bi(t,St,i,It,i)⁠. The representation summarizes the forward option value variation in terms of observable variables: the calendar timet⁠, the underlying security forward priceSt,i⁠, and the option’s own implied volatilityIt,i⁠. The progression of time is deterministic. The security price and the option’s implied volatility represent the two observable sources of random variation in the option price.

The BMS model was originally proposed as a bottom-up option pricing model with an assumed geometric Brownian motion security price dynamics. Nowadays, both academia and industry have been using the model more as a value representation function and a quoting mechanism, which transforms the option price into a more stable and more cross-sectionally comparable implied volatility. This paper uses the model as a value representation function summarizing the observable sources of variation of an option contract. The representation is applicable to both vanilla options and exotic contracts, as long as one can find a strictly positive volatility input to the BMS value function to match the observed price of the contract. That input is the contract’s BMS implied volatility,It,i⁠.

Through the BMS representation, we can attribute the short-term profit and loss (P&L) of the option investment via a Taylor expansion of the value function,

d B i = [ B t i d t + B S i d S t , i + B I i d I t , i ] + [ 1 2 B S S i d S t , i 2 + 1 2 B I I i d I t , i 2 + B I S i ( d S t , i d I t , i ) ] + … , (1)

where we adopt the continuous time notation (⁠dBi,dt,dSt,i,dIt,i⁠) to denote the short-term changes in the forward option value, the calendar time, the forward security price, and the contract’s implied volatility, respectively.Equation (1)suppresses the arguments (⁠t,St,i,It,i⁠) of the value function and use(Bt,BS,BI,BSS,BII,BIS⁠) to denote its first- and second-order partial derivatives. It has become common industry practice to manage the risk exposures of derivative positions via these BMS sensitivities, commonly known as the option’s theta (⁠Bt⁠), delta (⁠BS⁠), vega (⁠BI⁠), gamma (⁠BSS⁠), volga (⁠BII⁠), and vanna (⁠BIS⁠). Furthermore, it is often convenient to represent the changes in security price and implied volatility in proportions to the security price and implied volatility levels, (⁠dSt,i/St,i,dIt,i/It,i⁠). In this case, one can represent the corresponding exposures in cash terms: cash delta (⁠BSS⁠), cash vega (⁠BIS⁠), cash gamma (⁠BSSS2⁠), cash volga (⁠BIII2⁠), and cash vanna (⁠BISIS⁠).

The first bracket inEquation (1)collects the first-order effects. The second bracket collects the second-order effects on random security price and implied volatility movements. When the security price and the implied volatility are expected to move continuously over the next instantdt⁠, continuous-time mathematics dictate that expansions to the second order are sufficient to capture the short-term option value variation. When one expects randomly arriving jumps in the security price and/or the implied volatility movements, one can expand the value function to higher orders of the corresponding random variable. This paper confines the expansion to the second order and leaves the analysis of higher-order effects to future research.

1.3 Decentralized pricing of a single option contract

By taking expectation on the P&L attribution inEquation (1)under the risk-neutral measureQ⁠, we can analogously attribute the risk-neutral expected excess return to each risk source,

Different from classic bottom-up option pricing models that generate cross-contract option pricing comparisons based on the security price dynamics specification, the decentralized pricing relation in(6)focuses exclusively on the risk exposures and risk magnitudes of the observable risk sources of a particular contract. The pricing relation requires that the time decay of the option contract balances out the risk-neutral expected gains from the contract’s exposures to the underlying security price and the option’s implied volatility movements. The contribution of each risk source to the risk-neutral expected excess return of the contract is given by the product of the contract’s exposure to that risk and the risk-neutral expected value of the risk magnitude.

Proposition 2 ( Decentralizing pricing of an option contract ). The decentralized pricing relation in (6) requires the time decay of the option contract be balanced by the risk-neutral expected excess return contribution from each risk source. Each risk source contributes to the risk-neutral expected excess return by the product of the contract’s exposure to that risk and the risk-neutral expected value of the risk magnitude. The risk-neutral ( ⁠ Q ⁠ ) expected excess return contributions (QRC) from the vega, gamma, volga, and vanna exposures are, respectively, Q R C = [ B I i I t , i μ t , i , 1 2 B S S i S t , i 2 v t , i , 1 2 B I I i I t , i 2 ω t , i , B I S i I t , i S t , i γ t , i ] , (7) with ( ⁠ μ t , i , v t , i , ω t , i , γ t , i ⁠ ) denoting the corresponding risk-neutral moment conditions on the underlying security forward return and the proportional implied volatility change.

Bottom-up option pricing starts with the same no-dynamic-arbitrage condition and an analogous pricing relation in terms of the option’s expected excess return attribution. This bottom-up pricing relation is often referred to as the fundamental partial differential equation. The difference is that the bottom-up attribution is not to an option’s own observable variables through a known functional form, but rather to some hidden states of an assumed dynamics through a yet unknown value function. This subtle difference generates significantly different implications. By attributing the return to some hidden states of an assumed dynamics, the pricing relation is not limited to a single option contract, but is generally applicable to all option contracts on the same underlying security, upon which the dynamics is specified. Meanwhile, as the attribution is through an unknown valuation function, one needs to integrate the attribution based on the assumed dynamics through the whole life of the option contract, in combination with the contract’s terminal payoff condition, to solve the valuation function. It is this integration process that creates the computational complexity. Option valuation under most bottom-up pricing models involves Fourier transforms or other forms of numerical integration (e.g.,Bakshi and Madan 2000;Duffie, Pan, and Singleton 2000;Carr and Wu 2004). It is also this integration process that heavily strains the dynamics specification. The specified dynamics must be correct through the whole life of the option contract to generate accurate pricing implications.

By contrast, by attributing the return to observable variations of a contract through the known BMS functional form, the top-down relation in(6)can be directly applied to price the particular contract. As the valuation function is already known, we do not need to integrate the differential relation to solve the valuation function, but rather we can simply use the relation to determine the pricing of the contract at that particular point in time based on the conditional expectations of the moment conditions at that time, with no direct reference to how the expectation and pricing will change in the future.

The BMS value function itself is highly nonlinear. The return attribution on the nonlinear value function acts as a first-layer nonlinear transformation. It aligns the expected excess return contributions from different risk sources in a linear, additive fashion. The pricing relation linearly aggregates the risk-neutral expected excess return contributions from the different risk sources to balance out the option’s time decay.

A key step of statistical analysis is to construct relevant features that can explain the variation of the target variable. In the top-down valuation framework, the choice of the value representation function dictates the set of features one constructs to explain the option pricing variation. By adopting the BMS value representation, we construct five features from the observation of a single option contract, that is, the option’s theta, vega, gamma, volga, and vanna. In the industry, practitioners use the same set of features to manage the risk of option portfolios (Bergomi 2016). In the academic literature, several studies also use some of these features to explain the option return variation (e.g.,Buchner and Kelly 2022;Fournier, Jacobs, and Orlowski 2024). The decentralized pricing relation in(6)establishes a linear, no-dynamic-arbitrage linkage between these five features for each option contract.

1.4 Unite decentralized risk with common market pricing

In contrast to bottom-up option pricing, which relies on the sophistication of a single security price dynamics specification to capture the pricing behaviors of all its option contracts, the decentralized pricing relation inEquation (6)starts with a very simple value representation function through the BMS pricing equation, but achieves great flexibility by decentralizing the pricing relation down to a single option contract. In fact, the pricing relation offers too much flexibility because an option’s time decay can be balanced by risk-neutral expected excess return contributions from four risk sources. Some centralization is necessary for identification.

Historical centralizing efforts often fall back to the old habits of assuming common risk dynamics across different option contracts. If one assumes that the risk-neutral moments (⁠μt,vt,ωt,γt⁠) are identical acrossNoption contracts on the same security, one can pool the pricing of these contracts and identify the common risk-neutral moments through a cross-sectional regression,

y t = Z t c t + e t , (8)

whereytdenotes the (⁠N×1⁠) vector of option time decay withyt,i=−Bti⁠,Ztdenotes the (⁠N×4)exposure matrix with

Z t , i = [ B I i I t , i , a m p ; 1 2 B S S i S t , i 2 , a m p ; 1 2 B I I i I t , i 2 , a m p ; B I S i S t , i I t , i ] , (9)

and the regression slope coefficients capture the risk-neutral moment conditions that are assumed to be the same across the different option contracts,ct=[μt,vt,ωt,γt]⊤⁠. In the literature,Carr and Wu (2016)assume common implied volatility dynamics across all contracts underlying the same security;Carr, Wu, and Zhang (2022)assume common implied volatility behaviors for option contracts at the same maturity; andCarr and Wu (2020)assume that the implied volatilities behave similarly for nearby option contracts with similar strike prices and maturities. Since different option contracts do behave differently, these commonality assumptions face the classic tradeoff between the need for identification and the need for differentiation.

In this paper, we do not assume any common implied volatility dynamics. To the contrary, we explicitly recognize the behavior difference of different option contracts, and propose to use historical moment estimators as model inputs to capture these differences. Specifically, as we observe the histories of the two variables on each option contract (⁠St,i,It,i⁠), we can construct the corresponding historical variance and covariance estimators (⁠v^t,i,ω^t,i,γ^t,i⁠). We use these historical estimators to capture the risk behavior differences of different option contracts, to proxy the corresponding market expectations under the statistical measureP(⁠vt,iP,ωt,iP,γt,iP⁠), and to anchor the break-even contribution of each risk source as highlighted inCorollary 1.

It is important to appreciate that these historical moment estimators act merely as a simple starting point for the statistical forecasts. The decentralized nature of the pricing relation makes it ideal for cross-platform collaboration. If some investors have a different set of statistical forecasts based on either different statistical constructions or different information sources, these alternative forecasts can replace the historical estimators as the starting point of their analysis.

With the historical moment estimators anchoring the break-even contribution of each risk source, investors can decide to add a premium or a discount to each risk contribution based on risk preferences, anticipated forecasting biases, or supply-demand imbalances. We do not impose any structural constraints on the size or sign of the premium or discount, but we require that the same premium or discount per each unit risk of each source be applied to all option contracts on the same security. We use this common pricing assumption to unite the decentralized pricing of all the option contracts on the security.

Although the risk exposures and risk estimates on each option contract are decentralized, the underlying risk sources across the option contracts on the same security are largely the same. If the same source of risk were priced differently across different option contracts, for example, with a premium on some contracts but a discount on others, investors could in principle perform statistical arbitrage trading against the pricing difference and make a profit on average. These statistical arbitrage trading activities will in the limit push the pricing of each risk source to converge to the same value across all contracts, as posited by the classic statistical arbitrage pricing theory ofRoss (1976).

Different from the common risk assumptions in earlier centralizing efforts, our common market pricing assumption for a given risk source is much more natural and generally applicable as it conforms with statistical arbitrage trading principles and basic economic rationale. In fact, most of the classic literature also embeds such an assumption either explicitly or implicitly. The difference is that the earlier centralizing efforts often combine common market pricing with common dynamics assumption to generate common risk-neutral dynamics across different option contracts. When the actual dynamic behaviors are different for different contracts, the common risk-neutral dynamics assumption amounts to imposing, albeit unintentionally, different market pricing on different contracts. Therefore, only after one explicitly recognizes and accurately accounts for the risk differences of different contracts, can one price these contracts with true fairness in proportion to their different risk levels on each of their risk exposures. The historical moment estimators serve as the basis for recognizing the historical behavior differences of different contracts, upon which we make the common pricing assumptions in proportion to the estimated risk levels.

The underlying idea is simple and general, but the exact specifications can vary depending on the exact form of the common market pricing assumptions. We make the following set of assumptions on the statistical expectation and common pricing of the different risk sources.

Assumption 1. Common pricing assumptions on decentralized risk estimates : (i) We assume no trend predictability on the implied volatility movements and set the statistical expectation on the proportional implied volatility change to zero, μ t , i P = 0 ⁠ , for all contracts. (ii) The difference between the risk-neutral and statistical trend on the proportional implied volatility change captures the volatility risk premium. We assume that the volatility risk premium on each option contract is proportional to the historical variance estimator on the proportional implied volatility change of that contract ω ^ t , i ⁠ , μ t , i = β V , t ω ^ t , i ⁠ , with a common market pricing coefficient β V , t per each unit of the volatility risk across all contracts i ⁠ . (iii) The risk-neutral variance of the security forward return and the proportional implied volatility change of each contract ( ⁠ v t , i , ω t , i ⁠ ) are assumed to be proportional to their corresponding historical variance estimators ( ⁠ v ^ t , i , ω ^ t , i ⁠ ), with common proportionality coefficients across all contracts: v t = β G , t v ^ t , i and ω t , i = β O , t ω ^ t , i for all i ⁠ . (iv) The risk-neutral covariance between the security forward return and the proportional implied volatility change of each contract ( ⁠ γ t , i ⁠ ) is assumed to be proportional to the square root of the product of the historical return and proportional implied volatility variance estimators, v ^ t , i ω ^ t , i ⁠ , with a common proportionality coefficient β A , t across all contracts: γ t , i = β A , t v ^ t , i ω ^ t , i for all i ⁠ .

The risk-neutral implied volatility driftμt,icontains both a volatility risk premium component and a market expectation component under the statistical measureμt,iP⁠. We make the simplifying assumption of no trend predictabilityμt,iP=0⁠, and assume that the volatility risk premium embedded in an option contract is proportional to the estimated implied volatility variance (⁠ω^t,i⁠) of that contract, with a common market pricing coefficientβV,tacross all contracts. The risk-neutral variance on the forward return and the implied volatility changes are allowed to deviate from their corresponding historical moment estimators by a common proportional coefficient,βG,tandβO,t⁠, respectively, either because of the pricing of some risk sources or because of mismatches between market expectations and the historical estimates. Finally, to generate the risk-neutral covariance, we do not directly apply a common proportional coefficient to the historical covariance estimates, but apply a common proportional coefficient to the square root of the product of the return and implied volatility variance estimators. This allows the presence of the nonzero risk-neutral covariance even when the historical covariance estimator is zero.

Applying this set of common pricing assumptions allows us to construct a linear option pricing model that unites the decentralizing pricing of individual option contracts on the same security.

(11) Plugging these risk-neutral moments into the decentralized pricing relation in (6) results in the linear option pricing model in (10) that unites the decentralized pricing of all option contracts. ▪

The following corollary sets the null values for the common coefficients under zero risk premium and zero forecasting bias:

Corollary 2. Under the null hypothesis of zero risk premium, zero implied volatility trend predictability, and zero common forecasting bias on the historical variance estimators, the null values of the common proportionality coefficients β 0 are: β V 0 = 0 , β G 0 = 1 , β O 0 = 1 , β A 0 = ρ ^ t , (12) where ρ ^ t represents the average historical return-implied volatility correlation estimator.

AsβV,tcaptures the common market pricing of the volatility risk, its null value is zero under the hypothesis of zero risk premium. If there is neither risk premium nor forecasting bias, the risk-neutral return and implied volatility variance should be equal to their corresponding historical variance estimators, with the null values on their proportional coefficients (⁠βG,t,βO,t⁠) being one. Under this null hypothesis, the risk-neutral covariance should also be equal to the historical covariance, which can be constructed as the product of the average correlation estimatorρ^tand the square root of the product of the variance estimatorsv^t,iω^t,i⁠.

Classic bottom-up pricing models centralize the pricing of all option contracts by specifying the security’s risk-neutral price dynamics. The dynamics specification sets structural limits on the market pricing of each risk source. It also excludes cross-contract arbitrage from bottom up. Any deviations from the model valuation represent strict arbitrage opportunities under the model environment. By contrast, our model centralizes the pricing of option contracts from top down by requiring that the decentralized pricing relations across different option contracts share the same market pricing on each risk source. Without a bottom-up structural specification, the deviations from the common pricing assumption do not necessarily represent arbitrage opportunities in any structural sense, but rather statistical arbitrage opportunities that should be profitable on average.

Without specifying the underlying security price dynamics and risk preferences, our top-down approach does not offer much theoretical guidance on the sign or size of the common premium or discount that investors charge on each risk source. When the common coefficient estimates deviate from their null values, the deviation can be driven by either rational market pricing or irrational mispricing of the underlying risk source under some structural assumptions. It can also be driven by mismatches between the market expectations priced in these option contracts and our zero trend predictability assumption or the historical moment estimators we use. Henceforth, without differentiating the exact underlying sources, we label the deviation of each coefficient from its null value as themarket price of riskfor that risk source. Specifically, we label the vector

η t = β t − β 0 = [ β V , t , β G , t − 1 , β O , t − 1 , β A , t − ρ ^ t ] (13)

as the market price of vega risk, gamma risk, volga risk, and vanna risk, respectively.

In the literature, the term “market price of risk” tends to be used in a structural model setting representing the risk premium per unit risk along a certain risk dimension. This paper uses the terminology more broadly as the deviation of the model coefficient estimates from their corresponding null values constructed under the assumptions of zero implied volatility predictability and zero forecasting bias from the historical moment estimators. The deviation can indeed reflect the market pricing of a risk dimension, but it can also contain other elements or biases induced by violations of the assumptions under which the null values are constructed. In particular, we assume zero implied volatility trend predictability and attribute the risk-neutral driftμt,iall to volatility risk premium. In practice, investors are bound to spend efforts trying to forecast the future direction of implied volatility movements. When the market expects a nonzero implied volatility trend, we would attribute this nonzero expectation to volatility risk premium, biasing the identification of the market price of vega risk. Furthermore, we assume that the historical variance and covariance estimators accurately reflect the corresponding market expectations. Although these historical estimators represent reasonable starting points for the corresponding forecasts, the market can in principle embed other sources of information to generate expectations that deviate from these historical benchmarks. Such deviations will also introduce biases in the market price of risk estimates.

The zero trend predictability assumption and the historical variance and covariance estimators represent simple benchmarks to examine whether our pricing framework is useful under these simplifying assumptions. With more accurate trend and variance/covariance forecasts, one can in principle obtain more accurate market price of risk estimates. The decentralized nature of the framework dictates that one can readily incorporate statistical forecasts from any other sources and directly examine their market price of risk implications.

The literature often represents the option pricing behavior in terms of the option implied volatility. The following proposition converts our linear option pricing model in(10), which is in the expected excess return space, into a representation in the implied variance space for vanilla options.

Under the implied variance representation in(14), the implied variance of the option contractIt,i2converges to the risk-neutral return variancevt,i=βG,tv^t,ias the moneyness (⁠zt,i+⁠) and maturity (⁠τt,i⁠) approach zero. The pricing relation adjusts the implied variance along the term structure dimension for the volatility risk premium(βV,tω^t,i)and along the moneyness dimension both linearly in(zt,i+)for the return-volatility covarianceβA,tv^t,iω^t,iand quadratically in (⁠zt,i+zt,i−⁠) for the implied volatility varianceβO,tω^t,i⁠.

The implied volatility variance estimator (⁠ω^t,i⁠) can vary across option moneyness and maturity. The variation contributes to the variation of the implied variance surface. Furthermore, the common pricing coefficients provide broad adjustments to the implied variance surface: The coefficientβG,ton the gamma exposure adjusts the overall implied variance level; the coefficientβV,ton the vega exposure adjusts the overall implied variance term structure; and the coefficientsβA,tandβO,ton the vanna and volga exposures adjust the implied variance slope and curvature in moneyness.

It is a direct benefit of the top-down pricing approach that we can use the historical moment estimators as anchors of the option pricing behavior. In a bottom-up option pricing model with an underlying security price dynamics specification, if we want to incorporate the information in the historical moment estimators, we would need to go through the pricing model first as a filtering process and generate the model’s implications on the forward price and option implied volatility behaviors as a function of some hidden state variables. The filtering process can only incorporate part of the behaviors of the moment estimators compatible with the dynamics specification, while leaving most of the rest out. In fact, most bottom-up model specifications are highly stretched already to match the option pricing behavior, leaving little room to accommodate the historical moment behaviors. As a result, in practical applications, the risk-neutral pricing efforts are often disintegrated from efforts in risk management and P&L attribution. Although a well-designed bottom-up option pricing model can generate reasonable pricing performance, the fitted dynamics often look nothing like real behaviors. Risk management often needs to rely on the calculation and discretionary aggregation of BMS sensitivities.

1.6 Cross-sectional regression identification of the linear pricing model

The linear option pricing model unites the decentralized pricing relations of individual option contracts on a security by assuming a set of common market price of risk coefficients per each unit of the individualized risk estimates on each of the decentralized risk sources. At each datet⁠, a cross-sectional regression identifies the common market price of risk coefficients,

y t = X t b t + e t , (19)

where the dependent variable is the time decay of the options withyt,i=−Btiand the explanatory variables are the BMS sensitivities adjusted by the corresponding risk estimates, capturing the expected excess return contribution from each risk source, with itsith row given by

X t , i = [ B I i I t , i ω ^ t , i , 1 2 B S S i S t , i 2 v ^ t , i , 1 2 B I I i I t , i 2 ω ^ t , i , B I S i S t , i I t , i v ^ t , i ω ^ t , i ] . (20)

The BMS sensitivities can be readily computed from the BMS value representation. For vanilla options, these sensitivities are all in simple forms as shown inEquations (16)–(18). The historical variance estimators (⁠v^t,i,ω^t,i⁠) can be constructed with the observed history of the security forward return and the option implied volatility changes at similar moneyness and maturities.

The slope estimates capture the common proportionality coefficients,

b t = [ β V , t β G , t β O , t β A , t ] ⊤ , (21)

with the null values given inCorollary 2as,

b t 0 = [ 0 a m p ; 1 a m p ; 1 a m p ; ρ ^ t ] ⊤ . (22)

The deviation of each coefficient estimate from the corresponding null value represents the time-tmarket price of risk estimate for the corresponding risk source:

η t = b t − b t 0 . (23)

The market price of risk estimates tell us whether investors value a risk dimension at a premium or discount, and by how much, relative to the break-even contribution of each risk source and in proportion to the individualized risk estimate of that risk source for each contract.

The residualetfrom the cross-sectional regression captures the idiosyncratic pricing deviation of the option contracts from the common market pricing assumptions, and therefore constitutes statistical arbitrage opportunities. An overpriced contract has faster time decay than the expected excess return contributions from the risk sources, generating a positive regression error. One can in principle buy contracts that are priced less expensively than the common pricing and sell contracts that are priced more expensively, and expect to make a profit on average.

Since the time decay for a vanilla option is half the cash gamma weighted implied variance,−Bti=12BSSiSt,i2It,i2⁠, the regression on the option time decay in(19)amounts to fitting a relation on the implied variance with cash gamma weighting. The weighting allows one to compare the different option contracts not in terms of their option price or implied volatility levels, but in terms of their contributions to the expected excess return on the forward notional.
article.2. Data and Estimation on Currency Optio:
The option pricing model is generally applicable to any types of options on any types of securities, as long as one can compute the BMS implied volatilities and the BMS sensitivities of the option contracts, and sufficient historical observations on the option implied volatilities and the underlying security returns are available to construct the corresponding historical moment estimators. We examine the model’s implications using over-the-counter currency option implied volatility quotes on the dollar prices of Japanese yen and British pound. Other researchers have been exploring the model’s implications in stock index and individual stock options (e.g.,Wu and Xu (2023)).

We collect over-the-counter floating option implied volatility quotes on the two currencies at a grid of five maturities at 1, 3, 6, 9, and 12 months, and five moneyness per each maturity at 10, 25, 50, 75, and 90 put delta. The implied volatility quotes and the corresponding currency spot and forward prices are available on Bloomberg. Our sample starts on January 24, 1996, and ends on October 13, 2022, spanning over 26 years.

The over-the-counter currency option contracts are executed with the underlying delta hedge. The industry convention is to quote the delta in absolute percentages. We treat the delta quote as forward delta to infer the strike prices and compute the BMS sensitivities. AnInternet Appendixprovides detailed explanations on the industry quoting convention and the procedure to infer the strike price and compute the option value and sensitivities from the implied volatility quote.

Each panel contains 25 lines depicting the time series across the grid of five delta and five maturities. Underlying each currency, the implied volatilities of the different option contracts move closely together. They tend to spike up during crisis times and calm down slowly afterwards. Such crises happen at different times for different currencies, with the 2008-2009 financial crises being a common large shock.

The pound option implied volatilities have lower average levels and show little time-series movements or cross-sectional dispersion during the first half of the sample, suggesting that the pound returns were not as volatile and the implied volatility surfaces on the pound options were fairly flat during that period. After 2008, however, the implied volatilities on pound options become higher in levels, and show larger movements, more frequent spikes, and wider dispersions across contracts, reflecting the end of an era with stable market conditions for the dollar price of pound.

In particular, the yen options have been showing a persistently positive skew whereas the pound options have been showing a persistently negative skew.

Figures 1and2highlight the three major sources of variation in the implied volatility surface, that is, the implied volatility level and its slopes in maturity and moneyness. Our option pricing model attributes the different variations in the implied volatility surface to different sources of risk exposures, that is, the implied variance level variation via the gamma exposure, the term structure variation via the vega exposure, and the implied volatility slope and curvature variation in moneyness via the vanna and volga exposure, respectively.

2.2 Historical moment estimators

We construct historical variance and covariance estimators based on the daily histories of the forward currency prices and the option implied volatilities. The model uses these historical estimators to proxy market expectations under the statistical measureP⁠. These estimators need to be constructed on the forward currency return and proportional implied volatility changes for each option contract, but the over-the-counter quotes are floating quotes at fixed delta and time to maturities. At each datet⁠, we interpolate that day’s floating quotes to generate the implied volatilities and forward prices for the previous business day’s contracts to construct the forward returns and proportional implied volatility changes on these contracts.

Specifically, from datet’s floating forward price quotesSt(τ)on a grid of fixed time-to-maturitiesτ⁠, we linearly interpolate in the log forward price space to obtain datet’s forward prices for the previous business day’s contracts,St(τ−Δt)⁠, withΔtdenoting the daily time interval. By holding the forward contract for one day, the maturity of the contract shrinks byΔt⁠.

For each of previous business day’s option contracts at fixed delta and time-to-maturityτ⁠, we infer the strike priceKof that contract, and interpolate datet’s floating implied volatility quotes to obtain the implied volatility level for that contract at strike priceKand the shrunk time to maturityτ−Δt⁠. First, at each delta, we linearly interpolate the total implied variance across maturity, assuming piecewise constant forward implied variance term structure, to generate the implied volatility atτ−Δt⁠. Then, at each interpolated maturity, we perform cubic spline interpolation to obtain the implied volatility at the required strike price. TheInternet Appendixprovides further details on the interpolation procedure.

With the interpolated forward prices and implied volatilities for previous business day’s contracts, we construct the daily forward return,St(τ−Δt)/St−Δt(τ)−1⁠, and the proportional implied volatility change,It(K,τ−Δt)/It−Δt(K,τ)−1⁠, for each option contract. Based on these daily return and implied volatility change histories, we construct the historical variance and covariance estimators (⁠v^t,i,ω^t,i,γ^t,i⁠) for each contractiover three historical horizons: 1 month, 1 quarter, and 1 year.

The model uses the historical moment estimators as an anchor and uses the cross-sectional regression to identify the common market price of risk for each risk source. For accurate identification, it is imperative that the historical moment estimators have low estimation errors to mitigate the errors-in-variables issue. To reduce estimation errors in the historical estimators, we assume that the moment conditions vary smoothly across moneyness and maturity and we perform local smooth averaging on the raw estimates with a bivariate Gaussian kernel. We represent maturity in the natural logarithm of time to maturity to give more resolution to options at shorter maturities and we represent moneyness in forward delta. As the risk behaviors tend to vary more across maturities but vary less across moneyness at the same maturity, we set the bandwidth in maturity to the default choice, but double the default bandwidth choice in moneyness to achieve a higher level of smoothing across the monyeness dimension.

We use the historical variance and covariance estimators to proxy the statistical expectation underP⁠.Wu and Xu (2022)show that the expectation is better represented by historical estimators of different horizons under different market conditions. At each date, we choose the horizon for the historical estimators based on their respective pricing performance on the option contracts. The horizon of the estimators that generates the best pricing performance matches the market expectation the best, and we use the estimators from that horizon as our estimators on that date.

The forward return variance estimators (⁠v^t,i⁠) only differ slightly across option maturity due to the forward price term structure variation. The implied volatility variance estimators (⁠ω^t,i⁠) can vary much more across both the option delta and maturity.Figure 3plots the average term structure of the implied volatility variance estimators, with yen options in panel A and pound options in panel B. The five lines in each panel represent the five options delta. The implied volatility variance estimators on yen options are on average larger and show larger variation across both option delta and time to maturity, consistent with their more dispersed implied volatility behaviors.

2.3 Delta-hedged option returns

We perform pricing and investment analysis weekly on every Wednesday of the week. When Wednesday is a holiday, we take the quotes from the previous business day. At a pricing datet⁠, based on the pricing results, we perform investment analysis on the options by initiating the investment on the next business day, holding the investment for a week, closing the investment on the business day after the next pricing date, and maintaining delta-neutrality throughout the investment with daily delta rebalancing.

The weekly delta-hedged excess return from writing an option contract is constructed as,

r t + 1 = 1 S t 0 ( B t 0 − B t n + ∑ j = 1 n Δ t j − 1 ( S t j − S t j − 1 ) ) , (24)

whereBtandΔtdenote the BMS forward option value and the BMS forward delta of the contract at timet⁠, and{tj}j=0ndenote the list of the business days over the weekly investment horizon witht0being the initiation date andtnthe closing date. We divide the excess profit and loss from the investment by the forward currency price level at initiationSt0to convert the excess profit into the excess return on the forward notional. Because of put-call parity, the delta-hedged excess return on forward notional is identical for a European call option and a European put option at the same strike and expiry. We usert+1to denote the delta-hedged option excess return over the next week.

At the initiation datet0⁠, we invest in the option contracts at the five maturities and five delta based on the pricing results on the same maturity and delta in the previous business day. To compute the investment returns, we track the pricing variation of these contracts over the week at each business date fromt1totn⁠, so that we can compute the option value change (⁠Bt0−Btn⁠) and the option forward delta variation (⁠Δtj−1⁠). We also track the pricing variation of the underlying currency forward contract to compute the profit and loss from the delta position,Δtj−1(Stj−Stj−1)⁠. We interpolate the floating currency forward curve and the floating option implied volatility surface as before to obtain the forward prices and implied volatility values of the invested option contracts.

article.3. Pricing Currency Options with the Lin:
We use the first year of daily data to generate historical moment estimators. We perform cross-sectional regression estimation of the linear option pricing model and investment analysis every Wednesday from January 29, 1997 to October 12, 2022. We estimate the model separately on yen and pound options and obtain two sets of market price of risk estimates.

3.1 Risk-targeting option portfolios

At each pricing date, for each currency, we perform a cross-sectional regression of the (⁠N×1⁠) option time decay vectorytagainst the (⁠N×4⁠) risk-adjusted risk exposure matrixXton theN=25option contracts across five maturities and five delta. The regression coefficientsbtcan be interpreted as the weighted average time decay of different option portfolios, each targeting a unit risk-adjusted exposure to a certain risk dimension,

b t , k = w t , k ⊤ y t , (25)

where the portfolio weight vectorwt,kon thekth risk-targeting portfolio is given by

w t , k = X t ( X t ⊤ X t ) − 1 1 k (26)

with1kbeing an indicating vector of zeros except for thekth element being one.

Figure 5plots the time-series averages of the portfolio weights for the four risk-targeting option portfolios. The different lines denote the weight variation across option delta at different option maturities: 1 (solid lines), 3 (dashed lines), 6 (dash-dotted lines), 9 (circle dotted lines), and 12 months (dotted lines). The average patterns of the portfolio weights are similar for yen and pound options. Their small differences are induced by the differences in the implied volatility levels and the historical moment estimates on the two currencies, with the former affecting the BMS sensitivities and the latter affecting the risk adjustments.

Open in new tab Download slide

The portfolio weights on the vega portfolio are similar across the option delta at each maturity. The absolute magnitudes of the portfolio weights at each maturity tend to be larger for at-the-money options than for out-of-the-money options. The weights are negative for short-dated options but positive for long-dated options, similar to the construction of classic calendar spread portfolios. The gamma portfolios are also largely calendar spread formulations, but in the opposite direction by being long short-dated options and short long-dated options.

The volga portfolios are constructed by being short at-the-money options and long out-of-the-money options, similar to the construction of the classic butterfly spread portfolios. By contrast, the vanna portfolios are long out-of-the-money call options and short out-of-the-money put options, similar to the construction of classic risk reversal portfolios.

In our linear option pricing model, the risk-adjusted exposure matrixXtis constructed based on the BMS summary of the major risk sources and the historical risk magnitude estimates. Each of the risk-targeting option portfolios constructed from the model targets a distinct risk dimension. The portfolio weights reveal classic patterns of commonly constructed option combinations in the industry. Indeed, broker dealers in the over-the-counter currency options market provide direct quotes on risk reversals and butterfly spreads. TheInternet Appendixoffers a more detailed explanation on industry quoting conventions in the over-the-counter currency options market.

3.2 Time-varying market pricing of risk

At each date, we construct the market price of risk estimatesηtas the difference between the regression slope estimatesbtand their null valuesbt0⁠, as shown inEquations (21)-(23). To compute the market price of vanna risk (⁠ηA,t⁠), we construct an average return-implied volatility correlation estimator across the option contracts for each currency and at each pricing date. As correlation tends to move more slowly than volatilities (De Santis and Gerard 1997;Frazzini and Pedersen 2014), we take the 12-month historical horizon to estimate the correlation.

The statistics inTable 2show both commonalities and differences between the two sets of market price of risk estimates.

Across the four panels, we see predominantly positive market prices of vega risk (panel A) and volga risk (panel C), in line with the strongly positive sample averages reported inTable 2. The time-series plots reveal some extreme estimates. In particular, even in our minimally structural setting, we would still expect the common proportionality coefficients on the gamma and volga riskβG,tandβO,tto be strictly positive so that the risk-neutral return and implied volatility variance stay strictly positive. Such a rationality constraint would require that the market price estimates on the two sources of risks (⁠ηG,t=βG,t−1andηO,t=βO,t−1⁠) be no lower than−1⁠; yet, the market price estimates on both risks fall below this rationality bound in a few days of the sample. Although rare, the market price estimates can indeed become irrational or implausible. During these rare occasions, it is very possible that some of these option contracts are severely mispriced or with large data error. We could have performed a constrained regression to force the estimates to be within some rationality bounds. Such constraints would transfer the common mispricing of a risk dimension to mispricings on individual contracts.

3.3 Variance contribution of different risk sources

To understand the contribution of each risk source to the expected excess return variation, we construct a variance contribution (VC) measure for each explanatory variable. From the cross-sectional regressionyt=Xtbt+et⁠, the fraction of uncentered variance ofytexplained by the explanatory variablesXtcan be written asbt⊤(Xt⊤Xt)bt/(yt⊤yt)⁠. We decompose this total explained variance into contributions from each explanatory variablekas,

VC k , t = [ b t ] k [ ( X t ⊤ X t ) b t ] k / ( y t ⊤ y t ) . (27)

which sum to the grossR2of the regression.

Table 3reports the percentile values of the variance contribution estimates from each variable. For both currencies, the highest variance contribution to the expected excess return variation comes from the gamma exposure, which varies from 57% to 90% for yen options, and from 65% to 89% for pound options within the 25th-75th percentile range. Indeed, had the option implied volatility not been moving randomly over time, the expected gains from the gamma exposure would be the sole source of variation to balance the time decay.

3.4 In-sample and out-of-sample pricing performance comparison

The model’s pricing performance is much better than existing bottom-up option pricing models. Because of the complex nature of bottom-up modeling and identification concerns, the common designs of bottom-up option pricing models are mostly confined to low-dimensional specifications. For example, the stochastic volatility models ofHeston (1993)andBates (1996)only have one stochastic variance factor. The stochastic skew models ofCarr and Wu (2007)have two stochastic variance factors. By comparison, our linear option pricing model embeds a four-dimensional risk structure to explain the implied volatility surface variation. The four risk sources capture variations in the implied volatility level, the implied volatility term structure, as well as the slope and curvature in moneyness. The linear risk structure makes the computation and identification extremely simple, and the high dimensionality naturally leads to superior pricing performance.

TheInternet Appendixperforms a formal pricing performance comparison with four bottom-up option pricing models, including two one-factor stochastic volatility models byHeston (1993)andBates (1996), respectively, and two two-factor stochastic skew models byCarr and Wu (2007). The one-factor stochastic volatility models generate mean absolute pricing errors of 82-86 basis points on yen options and 62 basis points on pound options. The two-factor stochastic skew models perform better than the one-factor stochastic volatility models and generate mean absolute pricing errors of 61-66 basis points on yen options and 49-53 basis points on pound options. The mean absolute pricing errors from our model are less than half of these estimates.

TheInternet Appendixalso considers an alternative linear option pricing model, which has the same dimensionality as our linear model, but follows historical centralizing efforts by assuming common risk-neutral moment conditions across different option contracts as discussed in(8),

y t = Z t c t + e t , (28)

where the dependent variableytis still the option time decay but the regressorsZt⁠, as in(9), are the BMS sensitivities without risk adjustments. The slope coefficientsctcapture the risk-neutral moments(μt,vt,ωt,γt)that are assumed to be the same across different option contracts.

The mean absolute pricing errors from this alternative specification are 44 basis points on yen options and 32 basis points on pound options. Its high dimensionality allows it to generate smaller mean absolute pricing errors than the low-dimensional bottom-up option pricing models; nevertheless, its performance remains significantly worse than our model, highlighting the importance of accommodating the different risk behaviors of different option contracts.

A bottom-up option pricing model tends to have both a set of model parameters that are assumed to be fixed and a set of state variables that vary randomly over time. TheInternet Appendixestimates the fixed model parameters of the four bottom-up models with a maximum likelihood method, and given the parameter estimates, extracts the variance rates as state variables at each date from the option observations up to that date with an unscented Kalman filter(Wan and van der Merwe 2001). Therefore, the reported pricing performance of these models is not only in sample, but also forward looking. The pricing of options at a given date not only relies on fitting the option observations up to that date via the Kalman filter, but also depends on the model parameters that are estimated using the whole sample of data. To examine the out-of-sample stability of the parameter estimates and investment implications, researchers necessarily need to estimate these models using an earlier part of the data and apply the parameter estimates to price and invest in options in later days (e.g.,Carr and Wu 2007).

Our model is quite different from these bottom-up models because it does not involve any fixed model parameters and has only a set of market price of risk coefficients, which can be regarded as state variables capturing the time variation in the market pricing of the option contracts. At any date, we extract the market price of risk coefficients from the option prices on that date through a cross-sectional regression, similar to the Kalman filter extraction of the state variables for bottom-up models. Naturally, such a model with no model parameters does not have any out-of-sample parameter stability issue, rendering classic out-of-sample stability tests on model parameters irrelevant. When one makes investment decisions based on the pricing errors and/or the market price of risk coefficient estimates from the regression, they are naturally out of sample because the regression identification only uses data on the pricing date and does not involve any data in the future. This feature also applies to the alternative linear model in(28)with common risk assumptions.

Without model parameter stability to worry about, theInternet Appendixconsiders an out-of-sample pricing exercise on the two linear pricing models from a completely different perspective. At each date, we randomly draw 80% of the data sample. We perform the cross-sectional regression estimation of the two models on this randomly drawn subsample. We then use the regression coefficient estimates to price all options on that date. We compute the mean absolute pricing errors in implied volatility on both the 80% in-sample data and the 20% out-of-sample data, respectively.

The exercise shows that our model experiences much smaller out-of-sample performance deterioration than the alternative model. The average out-of-sample deterioration is merely 5-6 basis points for our model, but 9-12 basis points for the alternative. As a result, our model’s outperformance against the alternative becomes even larger out of sample, with an average difference of 14 basis points or 74% for pound options, and 22 basis points, or 116% for yen options. As the common risk assumption clearly deviates from historical observations, the out-of-sample deterioration of the alternative specification is expected. By comparison, the out-of-sample analysis further highlights the general applicability and out-of-sample stability of our common market pricing assumptions.

Compared to commonly designed bottom-up models, our top-down linear option pricing model generates much better pricing performance, with much lighter computational burden, and without any out-of-sample parameter stability issue to worry about. These attributes are impressive, but even more impressive are its equally superior performance in explaining the options return variation with its high-dimensional risk structure, and its unique capability in predicting future option returns along the four risk dimensions with the corresponding market price of risk estimate from the model. The next section examines the model’s unique strength in explaining and predicting the options excess returns.
article.4. The Model-Implied Option Return Facto:
To demonstrate the unique strength of our linear option pricing model in both explaining and predicting options return variation, we construct a linear option return factor model in direct parallel to the linear option pricing model,

r t + 1 = X t f t + 1 + e t ϕ t + 1 + ε t + 1 , (29)

wherert+1denotes the (⁠N×1⁠) vector of delta-hedged option excess returns over the next week from writing the 25 options at timet⁠. Just as the option pricing model uses the risk-adjusted exposure matrixXtto explain the optionexpected excess returnvariation, the return factor model in(29)explains the cross-sectional variation of the optionrealized excess returnover the next week with the same set of risk-adjusted exposuresXt⁠. Furthermore, the return factor model expands the factor structure and includes the residualetfrom the pricing model to explain the option excess return variation due to the reversion of the pricing errors.

The slope coefficient vectorft+1on the risk-adjusted exposure matrixXtcaptures the future excess return on the factor portfolios that target a unit exposure to a risk dimension while being neutral to all other dimensions. The slope coefficientϕt+1on the pricing error represents the future excess return on a statistical arbitrage portfolio that targets a unit exposure to the pricing error while being neutral to all risk exposures.

Based on an IPCA analysis of the risk characteristics on stock index options,Buchner and Kelly (2022)find that the stock index option return variation can be explained by three to four factors, chiefly capturing the variations of the option implied volatility level and its slopes in moneyness and maturity. Our option pricing model captures the option value variation along similar dimensions through the BMS value representation: the implied volatility level variation via the gamma exposure, the implied volatility term structure variation via the vega exposure, and the implied volatility skew and smile variation along moneyness via the vanna and volga exposures. The return factor model in(29)inherits the high-dimensional structure of the option pricing model and adds the pricing error as an additional dimension to capture the pricing reversal behavior to fair valuation. Therefore, just as our high-dimensional linear option pricing model generates superior option pricing performance, we expect this parallel option return factor model to also perform well in explaining the cross-sectional variation of option realized excess returns.

We estimate the option return factor model on each currency at each week via a cross-sectional regression. TheR2estimates of the regression capture how well the return factor model explains the cross-sectional option return variation over the next week. The slope coefficients represent the weekly excess returns on the option factor portfolios that target each of the risk exposures.

The factor structure can explain the cross-sectional option excess return variation very well. The regressionR2estimates average around 88%–89%. In principle, every option pricing model implies a return factor structure; nevertheless, the low dimensionality of the commonly designed bottom-up pricing models severely limits their explanatory power in option return variations (Israelov and Kelly (2017)). Because of the limitations, a common industry maneuver is to adopt a bottom-up pricing model for valuation, but switch to BMS sensitivities for risk management and return attribution, thus disintegrating the pricing effort from risk management. Our top-down linear option pricing model uses the same BMS exposure as in most practical applications, and it directly embeds statistical moment forecasts to capture how the underlying security price and each contract’s implied volatility are expected to behave. The model generates exceptional pricing performance, and its high-dimensional risk structure also explains the options return variation very well. Therefore, for the first time we provide a modeling framework that can tightly and effectively integrate the pricing effort with actual profit and loss attribution and risk management.

Among the risk factors, the largest variance contribution comes from the vega exposure, which contributes to an average of 86%–92% of the delta-hedged option excess return variation. The vanna exposure also has a large variance contribution averaging around 9%. The average variance contribution from the volga exposure is close to zero, whereas the average variance contribution from the gamma exposure is negative at−10%for yen options and−15%for pound options.

It is interesting to observe that the variance contributions to the ex ante expected excess return variation are quite different from the variance contributions to the ex post realized excess return variation. Whereas the former captures the variation of the expectation, the latter captures the randomness of the return realization. From the pricing perspective, the time decay of an option is chiefly balanced by expected gains from the gamma exposure. From the perspective of the realized return variation, the key source of randomness from the delta-hedged option investment is the vega exposure and the implied volatility variation.

4.2 The excess return behavior of risk-targeting option portfolios

Table 4also summarizes the time-series behavior of the slope coefficients from the return factor model. The average excess returns on the different risk-targeting portfolios show different signs and different levels of statistical significance. For both currencies, writing the vega and volga portfolios generates significantly positive average excess returns. Writing the gamma portfolios generates negative average excess returns, statistically significant for yen options but insignificant for pound options. Writing the vanna portfolios generates negative average excess returns for both currencies, but neither average return estimate is statistically significant.

Across the factor portfolios, the most statistically significant is the statistical arbitrage portfolio that target a unit exposure to the pricing error from the option pricing model while hedging away all risk exposures. The portfolio writes overpriced options with positive pricing errors and buys underpriced options with negative pricing errors, while maintaining neutrality to all the four dimensions of the risk-adjusted exposures. The performance of this value-investing strategy depends crucially on the reverting behavior of the pricing errors. The high information ratio and the strong statistical significance validate that the pricing errors from our option pricing model are indeed highly reverting. This pricing reversion behavior on average explains an additional 3% of the option return variation over the next week.

4.3 Predicting risk-targeting portfolio returns with market pricing estimates

If our zero implied volatility trend assumption does not deviate too far from reality and the historical moment estimators provide a reasonably accurate representation of the market expectation under the statistical measureP⁠, the market price of risk estimates from the option pricing model should reflect the expected excess return on the corresponding risk-targeting portfolio. To examine the relation between the ex ante market price of risk estimate of each risk source and the ex post realized excess return on the corresponding risk-targeting portfolio, we perform a forecasting time-series regression on each risk-targeting portfoliok⁠,

f t + 1 , k = a k + b k η t , k + ε t + 1 , k , k = 1 , 2 , ⋯ , 4 , (30)

whereηt,kdenotes the ex ante market price of risk estimate of thekth risk, obtained from the cross-sectional regression estimation of the option pricing model in(19)on datet⁠, andft+1,kdenotes the realized excess return over the next week on the corresponding risk-targeting portfolio, obtained from the cross-sectional forecasting regression on the option return factor model in(29). We represent the excess returnft+1,kin annualized percentages to match the scale of the market price of risk estimate. Under the expectation hypothesis that the market price of risk estimate reflects the expected excess return on the corresponding risk-targeting portfolio, the time-series forecasting regression in(30)should generate an intercept of zero and a slope of one.

Thet-values in parentheses are against the null value of zero. The average bias estimates are positive for some portfolios and negative for others, mostly with low statistical significance. Of the eight intercept estimates on the two currencies, only two estimates show statistical significance.
