Despite the inherently large noise in return predictive regressions, the estimation results on the forecasting return regressions in(30)are broadly in line with the expectation hypothesis. The ex ante market price of risk estimate predicts the subsequently realized excess return of the risk-targeting portfolio with little average bias and with a slope close to one. The results suggest that the simple historical moment estimators provide a reasonably accurate anchor, not only for accommodating the different risk behaviors of different option contracts, but also for identifying the market price of each risk source relative to its break-even contribution.

4.4 Harvesting time-varying risk premium via factor timing

As the market price of risk estimates represent largely unbiased expectations of the subsequently realized excess returns on the corresponding risk-targeting option portfolios, investors can harvest the time-varying risk premium through factor timing based on the market price of risk estimates. When the market price of risk estimate is highly positive at a given date, it means that the corresponding risk-targeting portfolio is priced expensively relative to its break-even benchmark, and investors can expect to make a positive excess return by shorting the option portfolio. When the estimate is highly negative, the risk-targeting portfolio is priced at a discount relative to the break-even benchmark and investors can expect to make a positive excess return by taking a long position.

This section considers a simple factor-timing investment exercise. At each date, we transform the market price of risk estimate from per unit risk to per unit dollar notional. We then set the notional investment in each portfolio in proportion to its market price of risk per unit notional,

n k , t = c t η k , t , (31)

where the proportionality coefficientctrepresents a simple normalizing coefficient. We choose a single proportionality coefficient at each datectacross the four risk-targeting portfolios to normalize the average notional investment in the four portfolios to $100. Importantly, the investment decision at each datetdepends purely on the market price of risk estimates on that date, which are obtained from a purely cross-sectional regression of the options time decay against the risk-adjusted BMS exposures on that date. The investment decision does not involve any rolling forecasting return regressions, not does it incur any forward looking bias.

Historically, researchers have proposed many empirical return factor models across different markets and have designed investment strategies based on the estimation of these return factor models. The general strategy is to be long a factor portfolio if the historical average of the excess returns on the portfolio is significantly positive, and short if its historical average excess return is significantly negative. For example, based on the estimation results on the option return factor model inTable 4, such a strategy would propose to be long the vega and volga portfolios going forward as the historical average excess returns on the two factor portfolios are significantly positive. The issue with this approach is that the significantly positive average excess return estimates only reflect the historical behaviors of the factor portfolios. As is well-documented and frequently reminded, historical performance does not necessarily carry into the future. This is especially true when the risk premium on a risk dimension is strongly time varying and switches signs.

By contrast, estimating our pricing model through a cross-sectional regression on any date directly generates the market price of each risk source on that date. The market price of risk estimate represents the conditional expected excess return on the corresponding risk-targeting portfolio, and can be directly applied to the market timing investment of the portfolio without any forecasting regression involved. This factor timing generates highly profitable out-of-sample investment performance. In particular, strong time variation in the risk premium of a risk dimension does not reduce the efficacy of this market timing strategy, but rather makes it all the more useful relative to the traditional investment strategy based on historical averages of return forecasting regressions. This is an important and unique dimension of our model absent from prior literature. Investment based on empirical return factor models can only rely on historical averages and hope for the estimated historical risk premium to continue into the future. Estimating bottom-up models can sometimes generate some static market price of risk coefficients, but they are often much too stylized to be of any practical use for return prediction and investment decision.
article.5. Concluding Remarks:
Classic option pricing starts with bottom-up dynamics specification that centralizes the option valuation and guarantees cross-sectional consistency across all option contracts underlying the same security. The recent effort in top-down decentralized pricing relinquishes the emphasis on cross-sectional consistency but strives to lever the domain expertise of investors and value each option contract based on its own risk behaviors. This paper brings the literature to a full circle. It proposes to unite the decentralized pricing relations of individual option contracts, not by imposing common risk dynamics, but by imposing common pricing on each risk source in proportion to their decentralized risk magnitude estimates.

History tends to repeat itself in cycles of centralization for consolidation and decentralization for flexibility. Our model builds a bridge between the two efforts through the sequential application of two types of arbitrage arguments. We start top down by imposing the no-dynamic-arbitrage condition between a single option contract and its underlying security. This condition leads to a decentralized pricing relation on each individual option contract. We then propose to exclude statistical arbitrage opportunities from the decentralized pricing relations across different option contracts by requiring the same source of risks be priced the same across all option contracts on the same security in proportion to their estimated risk levels. The result is a simple linear option pricing model that generates significantly better pricing performance than commonly designed bottom-up models. Its underlying high-dimensional linear risk structure also explains the options excess return variation very well. The model thus for the first time successfully and tightly integrates the option pricing effort with actual profit and loss attribution and risk management. Furthermore, the market price of risk estimates from the cross-sectional regression estimation of the model strongly and positively predict the future realized excess returns of the corresponding risk-targeting option portfolio in line with the expectation hypothesis. This feature represents an important dimension of the model completely absent from prior literature.

The underlying idea of our model is generally applicable to a wide range of asset classes and different types of derivative securities. Future research should examine its pricing performance, risk management, and investment applications in other asset classes. Within the general framework, researchers can also explore different value representations to better summarize the relevant risk sources, and different common market pricing specifications to better highlight the pricing commonalities and the underlying risk drivers. Finally, while this paper imposes common pricing for options on the same security, how the market pricing varies across different underlying securities can be an interesting and economically important question for future research.
